question.text: In a reverse mortgage arrangement, when does the loan become due and payable?

answer.text: When the loan tenure of 20 years expires
When the property value falls below the outstanding loan amount
When the last surviving borrower dies or permanently moves out of the property
When the borrower fails to maintain the property in good condition

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The reverse mortgage loan becomes due and payable only when the last surviving borrower dies or permanently moves out of the property. Even after the loan tenure ends (maximum 20 years) and periodic payments stop, the borrower can continue living in the property. Interest keeps accruing on the outstanding amount, but repayment is triggered only by death or permanent departure. **The Trap:** Many candidates confuse the end of loan tenure (when payments stop) with the loan becoming due. The tenure ending only stops the periodic payments - it does NOT trigger loan repayment. This distinction is critical. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series X-A covers retirement product features where the loan repayment trigger is a key concept. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Reassure senior clients that they will NEVER be asked to leave their home during the reverse mortgage, regardless of tenure or loan amount. The property settlement happens only after their lifetime or permanent relocation.
